questions.question: What is the strongest starting point for a redesign after an incident?
options: Identify the hidden assumption that failed and the path that amplified the incident.
Replace every dependency used in the original system.
Assume the outage was just bad luck.
Skip detection details and focus only on final architecture.
questions.explanation: A useful redesign starts from the mechanism that made the failure possible and severe.

questions.question: Why is blast-radius analysis central to post-incident redesign?
options: Because the redesign should ideally stop the same failure from spreading as far next time.
Because blast radius matters only for compliance reviews.
Because large outages mean capacity planning was the only issue.
Because blast radius is unrelated to retries or propagation.
questions.explanation: Containment is often the clearest measure of whether the redesign helped.

questions.question: Why is fixing only the final symptom usually insufficient?
options: Because the same broken assumption or amplification path can trigger a similar incident again through a different surface.
Because symptoms never matter operationally.
Because incidents should always lead to full rewrites.
Because postmortems should avoid architecture changes.
questions.explanation: Strong redesigns address the mechanism, not just the visible result.

questions.question: Which signal best validates that a retry-storm redesign worked?
options: Retry-budget consumption and downstream load under partial failure, paired with user-facing error behavior.
Only average code coverage percentage.
The number of dashboards created after the outage.
The size of the on-call rotation.
questions.explanation: You want evidence that overload amplification is now bounded.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of stronger rollout gates?
options: They reduce unsafe propagation, but can slow convergence and add operational friction.
They eliminate the need for rollback plans.
They automatically improve every latency SLO.
They matter only for mobile clients.
questions.explanation: Safety mechanisms usually introduce some speed or complexity cost.

questions.question: Why should operator visibility be part of the redesign?
options: Because faster explanation and targeted rollback can be as valuable as the architecture change itself during the next incident.
Because operators only need visibility after the system is fully stable.
Because good operators make metrics unnecessary.
Because user-visible incidents are unrelated to explainability.
questions.explanation: Incidents are handled by humans, so explainability is part of system design.

questions.question: If the failure came from promoting lagging state during failover, what should a strong redesign emphasize?
options: Promotion gates, freshness validation, and degraded behavior when a clean failover target is unavailable.
Disabling all failover permanently.
Only increasing storage retention.
Removing observability to simplify the system.
questions.explanation: Failover safety depends on validating the new authority, not just switching faster.

questions.question: Why should a post-incident redesign name what got worse after the fix?
options: Because stronger containment or validation often adds cost, latency, or workflow friction, and senior answers state that openly.
Because every redesign should avoid all new costs.
Because trade-offs are not relevant after outages.
Because the interviewer mainly wants optimistic outcomes.
questions.explanation: Credible redesigns explain both the benefit and the new tax.
